perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the "Confidential Information" of the Disclosing Party shall mean all information concerning or related to the business, operations, financial condition or prospects of the Disclosing Party or any of their respective Affiliates, regardless of the form in which such information appears and whether or not such information has been reduced to a tangible form, and shall specifically include (1) all information regarding the officers, directors, employees, equity holders, customers, suppliers, distributors, insurers, reinsurers, brokers, independent contractors, sales representatives and licensees of the Disclosing Party and their respective Affiliates, in each case whether present or prospective, (2) all inventions, discoveries, trade secrets, processes, techniques, methods, formulae, ideas and know-how of the Disclosing Party and their respective Affiliates, (3) all financial statements, audit reports, budgets and business plans or forecasts of the Disclosing Party and their respective Affiliates and (4) all information concerning or related to the Transaction; provided, that the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party shall not include (x) information which is or becomes generally known to the public through no act or omission of the Receiving Party and (y) information which has been or hereafter is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a source other than the Disclosing Party (or any of their respective Affiliates or their
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the "Confidential Information" of the Disclosing Party shall mean all information concerning or related to the business, operations, financial condition or prospects of the Disclosing Party or any of their respective Affiliates, regardless of the form in which such information appears and whether or not such information has been reduced to a tangible form; provided, that the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party shall not include (x) information which is or becomes generally known to the public through no act or omission of the Receiving Party and (y) information which has been or hereafter is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a source other than the Disclosing Party (or any of their respective Affiliates or their
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific inclusions of (1) through (4) defining examples of confidential information, we create an in-text contradiction. Section 2 references 'Confidential Information', but the core definition of what it constitutes has been significantly narrowed. This could lead to disputes over what is considered confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure of Confidential Information. Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a specific definition of 'Confidential Information', including enumerated examples. The changed text removes these specific examples, narrowing the definition. The contradicted text refers to 'Confidential Information' without a clear definition. Because the original definition provided specificity that is now absent, there is a contradiction, as the Receiving Party's obligations regarding what constitutes confidential information are now ambiguous, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to
perturbation.changed_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party or GreenWay may be entitled in law or equity,' we introduce a contradiction. The section still mentions 'irreparable damage' warranting an injunction, implying a right to full enforcement, but now it limits the remedies available to just injunctions and makes it harder to prove in the court of law. This creates ambiguity and reduces the options for remedies. This contradicts, since, even though there is an injunction, the remedies are limited.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit right to 'specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy', limiting the recourse to just injunctions. This directly contradicts the implied right to full enforcement suggested by the 'irreparable damage' clause, creating a strong in-text contradiction as it limits available remedies and potentially makes it harder to prove the case in court, as stated in the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, promptly after the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.changed_text: If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, promptly after the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party may retain all Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall return' to 'may retain', we create a contradiction. Section 2 states the Receiving Party will not disclose or use Confidential Information. Allowing them to retain it after termination, contradicts the spirit of the agreement regarding non-disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure of Confidential Information. Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the Receiving Party 'shall return' Confidential Information, creating a clear obligation. The changed text modifies this to 'may retain', giving the Receiving Party the option to keep the information. This directly contradicts the 'Nondisclosure of Confidential Information' clause, which states the Receiving Party 'will not...disclose or use' the information without consent. Allowing them to retain the information inherently conflicts with the obligation not to use it, creating a strong in-text contradiction as explained in the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Term. This Agreement shall continue indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: Term. This Agreement shall continue for 1 year.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the term from 'indefinitely' to '1 year', we create a contradiction with the nature of the project itself. The preamble describes ongoing 'discussions concerning the development of an ethanol processing facility,' which is likely a long-term project. Limiting the NDA to one year introduces a contradiction as sensitive information might be critical beyond that period.
perturbation.contradicted_text: PREAMBLE The Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party are currently involved in discussions concerning the development of an ethanol processing facility by Oregon Trail Ethanol Coalition (the "Transaction"). As a result of such discussions, the Receiving Party may have access to certain confidential information of the Disclosing Party and GreenWay Consulting, LLC ("GreenWay"). The Disclosing Party has entered into a nondisclosure agreement with GreenWay prohibiting disclosure of GreenWay confidential information, subject to the Receiving Party executing this Non-Disclosure Agreement. The Parties desire to enter into this Agreement in order to allow disclosure to the Receiving Party and prohibit disclosure of such information to any other party.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the agreement would continue 'indefinitely', while the changed text limits the agreement to '1 year'. This creates a direct contradiction because the preamble describes 'ongoing discussions concerning the development of an ethanol processing facility', implying a need for long-term confidentiality, which clashes with the 1-year term. The NDA's purpose, as stated in the preamble, is to protect confidential information, and limiting the term to one year directly undermines that purpose, creating an inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: Permitted Disclosures. Notwithstanding Section 2, Receiving Party shall be permitted to: (a) disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel, but only to the extent reasonably necessary in order for such party to prepare, conduct and execute and deliver definitive documents for the Transaction; provided that Receiving Party shall take all such action as shall be necessary or desirable in order to ensure that each of such persons maintains the confidentiality of any Confidential Information that is so disclosed; and (b) disclose Confidential Information to the extent, but only to the extent, required by law; provided, that prior to making any disclosure pursuant to this subsection, the Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party of the same, and the Disclosing Party shall have the right to participate with the Receiving Party in determining the amount and type of Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, if any, which must be disclosed in order to comply with applicable law, including, without limitation, state and federal securities laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Permitted Disclosures. Notwithstanding Section 2, Receiving Party shall be permitted to: (a) disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel; and (b) disclose Confidential Information to the extent required by law.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the limiting clauses, we introduce a contradiction with the initial purpose of non-disclosure. Originally, disclosures were 'only to the extent reasonably necessary' and required action to 'ensure confidentiality'. Now, the Receiving Party can disclose to officers/employees/counsel without any limitations. This weakens the non-disclosure aspect and contradicts the core intent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure of Confidential Information. Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes critical limitations and safeguards regarding the disclosure of confidential information, specifically the 'only to the extent reasonably necessary' clause and the requirement to ensure confidentiality is maintained. This directly contradicts the 'Nondisclosure of Confidential Information' section, which states that the Receiving Party will not disclose Confidential Information without prior written consent, except as otherwise permitted by Section 3. The modified Section 3 now broadly permits disclosures to officers, employees, and counsel without any limitations, thereby creating a strong and clear contradiction with the intent and obligations outlined in the 'Nondisclosure of Confidential Information' section. The omission creates a scenario where the receiving party can disclose information without the constraints present in the original text which directly violates the purpose of nondisclosure outlined elsewhere in the document.

perturbation.original_text: Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each of the Parties and their respective successors and permitted assigns.
perturbation.changed_text: Successors and Assigns. This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of each of the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and their respective successors and permitted assigns', while keeping Section 9 which expressly prohibits assignment, we create a potential contradiction. While no assignment is allowed, the removal suggests successors might still be bound, leading to confusion about the agreement's scope across different entities if the original parties undergo mergers or acquisitions. If no assignement is allowed in section 9, there shouldn't be successors mentioned
perturbation.contradicted_text: No Assignment or Delegation. Any assignment, delegation or attempted assignment or delegation of the rights or responsibilities established under this Agreement shall be null and void without the prior written duly executed consent by the party charged.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the agreement benefits the parties, their successors, and permitted assigns. The changed text removes 'successors and permitted assigns,' while Section 9 prohibits assignment. The explanation says that if no assignment is allowed in section 9, there shouldn't be successors mentioned. This creates a contradiction because, without 'permitted assigns,' the agreement's applicability to successors becomes unclear, conflicting with the prohibition of assignment in Section 9. This uncertainty is enough of a contradiction to warrant a YES.
